+8.8% EPS

Cash

$105.8M

Higher sequentially

RSR Run Rate

~$95M

Up slightly

Taking one look at the figures in the table above shows us that Napco is doing well, so the question becomes: Is it justifiable to keep a bullish position? I’m bullish on the business, less so on the price.

Why? Well, hardware isn’t a drag anymore—equipment sales grew almost 12% YoY, and I’m especially pleased that the segment’s gross margins are back above 25%. That’s not the same level as its historicals, but it’s a big step up from last year’s figure.

The company’s annual recurring service revenue (RSR) is also doing well. That’s because of the monthly RSR, which is up 11.6% YoY to $23.5 million, and it’s moved Napco’s expectations for its run rate up to $95 million. With these figures, we can see that the demand for its products, especially Starlink radios, is steadily increasing. Remember that Napco’s RSR still has gross margins >90%, and the 0.8 percentage point drop to 90.3% in Q1 is because the company is giving bulk pricing to bigger consolidators, so it gets bigger sales volumes. That’s normal, and I like it because it doesn’t affect the underlying compounders.

Leading on from that, we have profitability. I already mentioned RSR’s impressive margins, but the overall business did pretty well. Gross margin was up 70 basis points to 56.6%, operating income grew faster than revenue, and Napco’s EPS ($0.34) was a new company record for a first quarter. So, we have both of the business’s segments growing at double digits, and there is over $100 million in cash. Napco doesn’t have any debt, dividend coverage is almost guaranteed, and the company’s leverage looks great.

In my opinion, there isn’t much to criticize about how Q1 went, so I’m looking forward to what the next quarter will bring. I believe that the hardware recovery will continue in Q2, especially because of products like MVP. MVP is Napco’s “cloud-based access control platform,” and it integrates with the company’s locking hardware. Given that we’re seeing Napco trying to increase recurring service revenue, the new subscription model it is tying MVP to is a surefire way to keep more of those customer dollars within its ecosystem.

Since the MVP platform works for both enterprise and smaller businesses, I expect to see demand for it rise going into the next few quarters. However, I don’t think we’ll see any meaningful RSR contributions or lift from it until FY27 at the earliest.

It's Beyond Reasonable Levels

At today’s levels, I’d still say that Napco is near a full valuation. ~$40/share means that the stock is trading at 32x P/E TTM and ~26x EV/EBITDA TTM. Those are decent premiums over the sector medians, but in this case, let us look at the EV/Sales multiples instead. Napco currently trades at EV/Sales multiples (6.45x FWD) that have >100% premiums over its sector medians, and it’s even worse with the P/S ratios. I’m not sure that paying over the odds for this level of growth is the smart thing to do.

Yes, both revenue streams are growing at double digits, margins are up, and RSR is compounding nicely. I can certainly understand paying up for a capital-light, debt-free business with record EPS expansion, but it looks like the stock is running hot on sentiment, and it’s even dipped a bit after Q1 FY26 results came out.

The sentiment around it is still very bullish, though: Wall Street has it as a Strong Buy, and I’m sure they’re assigning a lot of value to its hardware recovery and operating leverage. Still, I prefer to sit and watch first. All things considered, I’m really pleased with how Q1 FY26 shaped up for Napco, and I’m confident in its long-term outlook. I just think that the current price is a bit too rich, and I’m not willing to assign a buy with such a high premium.

data.title: Meta Platforms: The Mag7 Bargain Champion Stock Ready To Outperform (Upgrade)
data.description:
Meta has been here before

This is going to mark the 4th time that I've covered Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) and the first time since 2022 that I consider the stock returning to "anti-bubble" status, regaining the crown as the cheapest stock amongst the Mag 7. The stock has come a long way since it was the most hated tech stock back in 2022. The company hit $99/share, and many analysts were predicting Meta's demise due to reckless spending on reality labs, the Metaverse and the augmented reality glasses:

For the good or the bad, the reality labs project put Meta ahead of many competitors in terms of buying Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs which became an extremely valuable commodity after LLMs debuted to the public. This allowed Meta to begin developing it's own AI systems and still remains one of the few ways investors can own a company that actually owns it's artificial intelligence IP. Other plays like Anthropic (ANTHRO) or OpenAI (OPENAI) are private. The most prominent access to AI intellectual property would be in Google's Gemini which is beginning to pull ahead in several benchmarks with the release of Gemini 3.0.

Let's take a look at the growth patterns over the last 5 years:

Good financial planners and analysts normally emphasize to their clients that stock prices in an efficient market follow earnings and revenue. This sell off in Meta has finally put the 5 year price growth below revenue and EPS growth. This measure of reflexivity was popularized by both Peter Lynch and George Soros. When the price appreciation is below earnings growth and still expected to grow into the future, this measurement alone is a good trigger to start a deeper investigation.

The main metric than now sits below price growth is free cash flow. This is going to be a trend across the Mag 7 as CAPEX spending is going warp speed for those involved in cloud computing and AI development projects requiring both GPUs and data center spend. Since free cash flow in simple terms is cash from operations minus CAPEX, greatly increased CAPEX will temporarily put a dent in free cash flow until that CAPEX pays dividends in the future.

Meta floats more debt

Then right on queue, rates started to drop, Meta and others in the hyper scaler category started to float new debt offerings to continue their AI CAPEX spending bonanza. This is something I pointed out in an earlier article which can be found here: "Big Tech's Credit Power Is Staggering, $Trillions Ready To Flow Into Data Centers."

This was posted September 26th of this, year mind you, before any of the borrowing by the Mag 7 started to commence. I made the observation that I believed the bears were early. The balance sheets of these companies still remain largely untapped when you do a credit analysis of their net debt [most have net cash positions] versus their EBITDA where companies can normally borrow up to 3 X their EBITDA before getting knocked out of investment grade.

The market didn't like Meta's new debt offerings regardless of earnings and revenue growth (emphasis added):

Since September, public bond issuance by four of the major cloud computing and AI platform companies known as "hyperscalers" has hit nearly $90 billion, with Google owner Alphabet, $25 billion in bonds, Meta $30 billion, Oracle, $18 billion, and Amazon, the most recent, $15 billion, according to Reuters calculations of publicly available data. Only Microsoft, the fifth one, has not tapped the debt market in recent weeks.

This is a shift in perception. Meta and others were given free reign to spend since they were "self funding" cash-spewing machines. They still are, the market had just never taken a look at these company's balance sheets to realize how underleveraged they all really are. If you had a client with the debt levels versus cash and cash flow of these companies you'd say they are either missing out on opportunities or they have been watching too much Dave Ramsey.

Let's not conflate these companies with Oracle (ORCL), either. The Mag 7 have much stronger balance sheets on average.

Year to date performance of the Mag 7

Year to date, Meta is one of the worst performers, which could have been expected entering this year after their epic run from 2022 until current [up 500% total return since Oct. 2022].

Performance of Meta versus its all-time high

With META now entering bear market territory down more than -20%, it's a great time to re-run some numbers and see how Meta's valuations now compare to the rest of the Mag 7.

First, we'll take a look at a modified Magic Formula ranking system which adds the EBITDA yield to the return on total capital. This system, originally designed by Joel Greenblatt, considers earnings yield [or in this case EBITDA yield] to be an indicator for cheap and ROIC or ROTC yield to be an indicator for quality. I like to use EBITDA for the Mag 7 because they have so many non-cash deductible items under depreciation and amortization.

Company EBITDA Yield % ROTC % Total Score
AMZN 6.88 10.38 17.26
META 8.03 22.00 30.03
GOOGL 5.24 20.32 25.56
MSFT 5.41 19.58 24.99
NVDA 3.06 67.06 70.12
AAPL 4.02 45.93 49.95
TSLA 1.01 3.35 4.36
Rank Company Score
1 NVDA 70.12
2 AAPL 49.95
3 META 30.03
4 GOOGL 25.56
5 MSFT 24.99
6 AMZN 17.26
7 TSLA 4.36

With tech companies, the ROIC or ROTC numbers tend to be the primary factor on where these rank, a lot of this can be altered by buybacks or cyclical trends. Nvidia is a cyclical king where the cycle just never seems to end. They have the largest market cap for a reason with a 67% ROTC. Meta sits third on this list, which is an improvement as they were previously top 2 or 3 most expensive when evaluating stocks in the Mag 7 with this methodology.

Cheapest on a pure forward EV/EBITDA basis

Then just purely looking at forward EBITDA, Meta at 12.26 X FWD EBITDA is now the absolute cheapest on the list of the Mag 7.

Why Meta has a bright future

  • Facebook is still the king of social networks and market places: With over 3 billion monthly users, this is the add revenue machine that never stops laying golden eggs.
  • Same goes for Instagram: with about 2 billion active daily users ("ADUs"), Instagram is still the king of short form content social media.
  • Whatsapp may be the future of communication: Another 3 billion ADUs on Whatsapp. In my opinion, this could be the future of communication. While cell towers may still be the source of internet data for phones in the future, I can imagine a world where phone calls become more and more standard via encrypted apps like Whatsapp. Don't think it can happen? This is pretty much already the norm in places like India and areas of the Middle East.
  • Meta AI, large language model ("LLM") and more: Meta owns its own LLM and AI models. They've spent like mad acquiring talent and it seems to be now paying off. Having recently experimented with the new Meta AI app, it is already generating short form video AI content approaching the quality of a Sora 2 or VEO 3.

Criticisms and headwinds

  • Too much misdirected CAPEX: This does not just go for Meta but all of the Mag 7. The CAPEX spend now being funded with debt offerings has become a worrisome issue for the market. Meta with the "Metaverse" roll out in 2022 was the poster boy in the eyes of the market for reckless spending and this brings back nightmares for some.
  • No cloud business, yet..: For all the CAPEX spend, Meta does not yet have a true cloud business. They may in the future, but this is a short coming where companies that own or partner with large language models want to help other businesses utilize these models through their data centers and cloud businesses.
  • Sora 2 and Open AI are gunning for Meta and Instagram: I would assume after all the Meta poaching of OpenAI talent that CEO Sam Altman would like to have the last laugh. Sora 2 which generates AI Instagram style content is a direct competitor to Meta's Instagram.
  • The Metaverse is questionable: Still not a big payoff here and the Metaverse Ray Bans have not yet caught on. This is a future story yet to bare fruit.

Still one of the best balance sheets in the entire market

Even with the spend down in the cash position, Meta is still only borrowing 56% of their EBITDA. This company has a long way to go before debt becomes a worry, in my opinion.

PEG ratio valuation

If Meta wraps up the year at the predicted $25.9/share estimate in EPS, let's see where a PEG ratio valuation sits if we take the bullish case of Meta hitting the high end estimates of $35.53 a share in 2026. That bullish case would be a growth rate of 37.26% on a per share EPS basis. Using Peter Lynch's advice from One Up On Wall St., I set an upper limit on the PEG ratio of 25 X earnings [assuming a 25% growth rate is sustainable].

Again the PEG ratio divides the P/E ratio by the growth rate minus the percent sign. A ratio of 1 or less is a great growth stock value. To reverse engineer this we can use the growth rate as the multiplier and the EPS as the multiplicand. Using 2026 high end numbers and 25 as the multiple since a high end growth prediction clears 25%, this would be 25 X $35.53 = $888.25. This again would be the bull case. If growth rates keep up and the market stays strong next year, Meta looks pretty undervalued if you are a Meta bull.

This $888 number sits just above the forward Wall St. Analyst average.

Risks and summary

The entire AI trade could blow up. That's the biggest issue here in my opinion. If the market rotates out of this trade, no company in the Mag 7 will be spared regardless of valuation. I believe each company is unique and has their strengths and weaknesses [Mag 7], I don't think a crash in Meta stock will be because of the company, but a cyclical trade out into other sectors.

The company looks very cheap to me with some great levers yet to be pulled. This is the first time I've considered adding shares to my position since 2022. Strong Buy.

data.title: VGLT: Long-Dated Treasury Exposure For Balanced Yield Risk
data.description:
The Vanguard Long-Term Treasury Index Fund ETF (VGLT) is a low-cost, fixed income exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to the long end of the Treasury yield curve. VGLT has a 30-day SEC yield of 4.36% with an average duration of 14.1 years. Given that the strategy focuses on the long end of the yield curve, investors can expect the strategy to be most impacted by expected economic conditions with significant sensitivity to Federal Reserve interest rate policy.

About Vanguard Long-Term Treasury Index Fund ETF

VGLT was launched by The Vanguard Group on November 19, 2009 as a low-cost, passively managed, long-term US Treasury portfolio strategy. VGLT has a net expense ratio of 3bps, in line with the State Street SPDR Portfolio Long-Term Treasury ETF (SPTL).

VGLT was designed to track the performance of the Bloomberg US Long Treasury Index, which includes US Treasuries with a maturity greater than 10 years. The strategy excludes treasury inflation-protected bonds ((TIPs)) and floating rate securities. The fund maintains a dollar-weighted average maturity that is reflective of the underlying Index.

VGLT pays out a monthly distribution rate annualized at $2.49/share, yielding 4.39% on a trailing twelve-month basis. The 30-day SEC yield, which measures the distribution yield in relation to the expense ratio, is 4.36%. The distribution rate will be reflective of the interest rate and yield of the Treasuries held within the portfolio strategy, which may vary across issuances depending on interest rate policy at the time of issuance and the bank subscription rate of the issuance.

The portfolio is primarily made up of long-term treasuries with 15-25+ years to maturity. The fund has a portfolio turnover rate of 19.8%, likely the result of managing the portfolio to maintain its aggregate maturity profile, which is an average effective maturity of 22 years.

The average duration of the fund is 14.1 years. For reference, the Vanguard Short-Term (VGSH) and Intermediate-Term (VGIT) Treasury ETFs have average durations of 1.9 years and 4.9 years, respectively. For virtually no duration risk, investors may consider the Vanguard 0-3-Month Treasury Bill ETF (VBIL). At a high level, duration is the relation between a bond’s price with respect to an incremental change in the interest rate set by the Federal Reserve. A higher duration means that the bond is more sensitive to interest rate changes.

Investors should be conscious of duration risk in relation to Federal Reserve interest rate policy, as it can significantly impact the price of the bonds. The price of a bond has an inverse relationship with the interest rate. An increase in the interest rate will generally result in a lower price for the bond and vice versa. If the interest rate is cut, the bond price is expected to increase.

Comparing VGLT to the Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), we can visualize the relationship between long-term Treasuries and the benchmark rate. When analyzing the chart, investors must consider that the market will price in expectations for interest rate policy throughout the cycles. This can particularly be visualized going into the initial rate increases in 2022.

Though interest rate policy influences long-term Treasuries, these bonds are generally more sensitive to expected economic conditions. Long-term Treasury yields may be reflective of investors’ expectations of future economic conditions, where yields may tighten going into periods of expected economic stress. At a high level, economic conditions may influence the Federal Reserve’s interest rate policy to a certain extent and in turn, influence the value of the long-term Treasuries held in VGLT; however, the market ultimately prices the bonds based on the expectations of the future economic environment and interest rate policy.

Investor Suitability

VGLT can be utilized for tactical positioning as part of a diversified fixed income portfolio strategy. Investors should take into consideration duration risk when evaluating whether VGLT is the right strategy for investment. Across the yield curve, investors may consider VBIL, VGSH, VGIT, or VGLT to balance duration risk, actively weighting duration based on their economic expectations. From an investment perspective, investors may also consider rotating positions across the yield curve in order to manage duration risk during different periods throughout the economic cycle.

Specific Risks Related To VGLT

VGLT invests in long-dated Treasuries, exposing investors to higher duration risk with respect to the short-end of the yield curve. The market’s expectations of interest rate policy set by the Federal Reserve as well as economic conditions may influence the pricing of VGLT and may not be reflective of the current market conditions.

Final Thoughts

VGLT can be utilized by risk-adverse investors seeking exposure to the long end of the Treasury yield curve. Investors may utilize other regions of the curve to spread risk as part of a diversified fixed income portfolio strategy.

This article answers these three main questions about VGLT:

  • What type of investor is VGLT best suited for?
  • Are there specific risks associated with an investment in VGLT?
  • How sensitive is VGLT to monetary and fiscal policy changes?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: S&P 500: Increased Volatility Calls For Prudence And Holding
data.description:
Currently, the S&P 500 has become, in historical terms, a clearly expensive asset. After a correction of close to 5% from recent highs, the index offers a typical picture of a slowdown in the last bullish phase, more typical of a digestion phase than a new momentum. All this is happening with a labor market that is beginning to lose traction, with the recent US government shutdown and a market that has had to readjust its expectations of short-term interest rate cuts downward. In this context, I believe that this is not the best time to “buy on the dip.” My recommendation is to maintain exposure to the S&P 500 without aggressively increasing positions and to wait for the upcoming employment data and new signals from the Fed on the path of short-term rates.

Inflation Expectations

In the graph above, we can see the inflation expectations in the USA. The green line has been stabilizing since 2023 (2% range), indicating to me that the market, at least in the short term, does not fear a run on inflation. The real premium risk line tells us that the premium demanded by investors for long-term real rate risk is currently at contained levels (around 1.25%), which also reflects that fixed-income investors are not asking for an excessive premium for future inflation risk.

The most interesting aspect is found in the variable inflation risk premium. The economy is now stabilized at the 0.3-0.4% level, indicating that the market is paying next to nothing for a future inflationary rebound. From my point of view, this seems to mean that the market believes that the Fed has inflation under control, which a priori may positively influence the development of the continuation of the upward trend of the SP500 once this correction period has passed.

Labor Market

The other key variable that both the Fed and the market monitor to gauge the temperature of the economy is the labor market. In this case, I propose the metrics of job creation and the working-age participation rate to see how they have evolved in recent years. As can be seen in both images, we see a decline in job creation (image 1), with 26,000 jobs lost in August and 119,000 jobs created in the September report. The trend is not very strong and seems negative for the first metric, but if we complement it with the second, we see that labor force participation among people of working age is at historic highs (83.7% for August). This leads me to believe that unless the trend in this second metric deteriorates, there is no reason to fear any shocks in the employment variable, even if it grows below established standards. The conclusion is very simple at this point: as long as people of working age continue to work, there is no reason to fear a shock in the labor market, even if it does not grow at the same rate as in previous years.

Interest Rates Perspectives

In the graph below, we can see the interest rate expectations for the FOMC meeting on December 10, 2025. A clear change in trend can be seen starting in October, when the market was discounting a cut for the next meeting with expectations of almost 100%. From that date onwards, we can see how the orange line (current interest rate range) has been recovering ground to reach a probability of 60%. In other words, expectations regarding the maintenance of interest rates in this range have gone from 4% on October 4 to 60% today. This change may be triggered by a recognition of the strength of the labor market and/or a recognition that the inflationary problem caused by monetary expansion in 2020-2021 has not yet been fully resolved.

Market Multiples

In terms of market valuations, we can see how Shiller's CAPE is clearly moving above its +1 standard deviation band, at levels only comparable to the major valuation peaks of recent decades, while the simple historical P/E ratio is also at the high end of the range, close to the highs seen in the dot-com bubble and post-COVID. This tells us that the market is willing to pay very demanding multiples for every dollar of future profit, in a context where the economic cycle is cooling and interest rate expectations have been tightening. For the thesis I defend, these charts do not point to a “bargain” after the 5% correction, but rather to an index that remains expensive and where the margin for multiples to expand further is limited. In this situation, any disappointment in growth or earnings has more downside potential than extra upside potential based on valuation alone.

This week's Nvidia results are a perfect example of how “market behavior” is hyper-focused on a few mega caps linked to the AI narrative. The company once again smashed expectations with record revenues of around $57 billion (+62% year-on-year), of which more than $51 billion came from the data center business (+66% YoY). This tells me that the index needs something more to continue rising, paying the current valuation multiples.

Market Behaviour

Analyzing market breadth, we find these three charts, in order: (1) new highs and lows, (2) stocks above their 50- and 200-period averages, and (3) stocks within 5% of their highs and lows, expressed as a percentage. Right now, only 30% of NYSE stocks are above their 50-day average, while around 55% are above their 200-session average. This tells us that in the long term, the structure is still bullish, but in the short term, most stocks are already lagging behind the market as a whole. We may be facing a one-off correction or the end of the bullish cycle mentioned above. If we look at the chart of stocks less than 5% from their highs or lows, we can see how at the beginning of the year there were many companies reaching their highs, but after the release day in April, they plummeted until more than 40% of companies were close to their lows. Today, we are seeing a brief trend where lows are rising and highs are falling, which is in line with the correction of recent days and with the thesis of keeping the index in the portfolio, waiting to see the development of the macroeconomic variables discussed in the first part of this article.

I would recommend that readers closely follow developments in inflation expectations, new employment reports, and the FOMC statement from the December 10 meeting, as well as earnings developments and market breadth, to see how everything fits together. If we see that the Fed's message is positive, that the labor market is responding with growth, and that inflation expectations remain at these levels, we are likely to see a healthy correction caused by excessive valuations of the index as a whole. If not, buckle up because a big drop is coming. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Ally Financial: Credit Improvement Defies Auto Loan Fears (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Ally Financial Inc. (ALLY) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 6%. While credit trends have shown signs of stabilization this year after challenges in 2023-2024, its capital and reserve positioning are both relatively light, which has limited upside. I last covered shares of Ally in July, rating the stock a weak “Hold,” expecting shares to be range-bound given its light capital posture. In hindsight, I arguably should have gone with a “Sell” rating, as the stock has lost 1%--however, I view this as close enough to flat as being consistent with the rating. With updated financials and a revised macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit ALLY.

In the company’s third quarter, Ally Financial earned $1.15 per share, which beat estimates by $0.14. The company continues to report improving credit metrics, which should help to alleviate concerns about its loan book. The auto loan sector has faced concerns about weakness among lower-income consumers as subprime auto delinquencies have reached a 25-year-high. Now, Ally is primarily a prime lender, and so its loan book has less direct exposure to this weakening.

As discussed at a conference in November, about 9%-10% of originations are to subprime borrowers, with just 2% to deeply subprime. Management reiterated its view that its own credit trends are improving, primarily thanks to tightened underwriting actions taken in late 2023. Management has seen its subprime borrowers struggling with inflation for several years, but policies are performing a bit better than modeled right now. As you can see below, with each passing year, vintages are performing better, reflecting the steady tightening of underwriting standards. This should keep the company fairly insulated from credit pressures.

In fact, we are seeing just that. In Q3, net charge-offs were 1.88%, which was down 36bps from last year. As a result, the company set aside $415 million for credit losses, down 36% from last year. Alongside this, we continue to see improvements in delinquencies. They are down 30bps from last year to 3.9%. There was a modest sequential tick-up, but this is consistent with seasonal patterns. I expect a ~10bp increase in Q4 and then a sizable decrease in Q1 as consumers typically use tax refund payments to catch up on past-due debts.

Frankly, the credit performance is very encouraging. That said, Ally needs to see continued improvement to justify its reserve positioning. While it has $800 million of “excess” reserves above normal levels, its auto loan reserve coverage is 3.75%, which is still less than 1x delinquencies and only about 2x charge-offs. This is relatively low, and I would like to see reserves closer to 3x charge-offs. As such, even as credit trends should continue to improve, I do not view reserve releases as likely.

Aside from auto loans, Ally has $4 billion of exposure to non-depository financial institutions (NDFI). This is primarily lender finance work for established private credit players. It budgets for 50bp annual losses, but so far this year, there have been no losses. A challenge for other banks in this space that caused losses was borrowers allegedly double-pledging assets. ALLY has been checking and has found issues with its collateral. Overall, this is a small share of the business, and it is performing appropriately. I am comfortable with the bank’s private credit exposure.

This improving credit quality posture is important as capital needs to continue to improve. Ally has a common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 10.1%, up 20bps sequentially. However, including unrealized losses, its CET1 ratio would be just 8.0%. This is up 90bps year to date, but it still is very low. Banks have time to phase these losses in, giving ALLY regulatory headroom, but this adjusted capital ratio is still relevant when considering the economic capital position of the bank.

ALLY should operate with ~9.5% CET1, and so it will continue to focus on building capital gradually to bring this adjusted CET1 up towards that level. Shares offer a 3.1% dividend yield, and I view this dividend as secure given earnings as ALLY continues to build capital. I do not expect buybacks until we see adjusted CET1 approach 9%, which will be a 2027 event in all likelihood.

This capital position has led to closer management of its balance sheet size (by reducing assets, its capital ratio improves, all else equal). Ally has $148 billion of deposits, which is down $4 billion from last year. Normally, I view falling deposits as a significant warning sign for a regional bank. However, in Ally’s case, this drop is deliberate. Given its weaker capital position, Ally has needed to work down its risk-weighted assets and shrink its balance sheet, which means it needs fewer deposits. As such, it has been a bit more aggressive on cutting deposit rates to have some attrition of high-cost balances.

This $4 billion decline in deposits is in keeping with its ~$5 billion drop in assets, so Ally has not needed to turn to wholesale funding to support its balance sheet (which is a negative for margins). Its loan-to-deposit ratio has been fairly stable throughout this process. Instead, Ally has appropriately calibrated its deposit actions for its efforts to reduce assets. Of course, a smaller balance sheet reduces earnings power, all else equal, but this is a necessary step to improve its capital posture, and I expect the balance sheet to be at or near its bottom with modest deposit growth likely over the next year.

Its cost of deposits was 3.5% in Q3, which was down 9bps sequentially and 73bps from a year ago. I like to see banks pass on 35%-45% of Fed rate cuts, but ALLY has been able to pass on over 70%. This is a very strong outcome, reflecting the fact it has wanted some deposit attrition. Because of this strong deposit pricing, ALLY has been expanding margins. Ally generated a 3.55% net interest margin (“NIM”) in Q3, up 10bps from Q2 and 23bps from last year.

On the other side of its balance sheet, originations were $11.7 billion at a 9.7% yield. This yield is actually accretive to its 9.2% portfolio yield, even with Fed rate cuts, reflecting wide risk premiums. Because of this, I expect loan yields to rise at least through Q1. Importantly, ALLY is not increasing credit risk to generate this extra yield, as 42% of originations came from its top credit tier. With Ally limiting loan growth, it can be choosier on the loans it funds, allowing further upward credit migration.

On the earnings call, management also detailed updated guidance. Ally now expects NIM to come in the top half of its range, while net charge-offs should be towards the low end at 2%. Its stronger deposit pricing actions and benefits from tighter underwriting standards are supporting results.

Back in July, I saw Ally earning $3.40-$3.75 per share this year. Given charge-off improvement, I expect results to come in at the top end of this range. Next year, we should see Ally earn at least $4.50 per share, given my view that credit trends should be fairly similar to this year and its improved margin position given the strong pull-through of Fed rate cuts. With its lower capital position and ongoing concerns about exposure, I see shares fairly valued in the 9-10x earnings area, or about $43.

That provides about 10% upside, alongside its 3% dividend yield. I view the consolidation is shares over the past few months as creating a more compelling buying opportunity, given the ongoing improvement in the business. That said, if you expect a recession, I would not yet add ALLY as that will increase credit losses. Given my view a recession is unlikely to occur, I am upgrading shares to "Buy" and would add Ally here.

data.title: Consider Preferreds When Seeking High-Yield Income With Less Volatility
data.description:
(This article was previously published for members of the Inside the Income Factory service.)

In this review of Preferred securities, I wish to discuss an asset class that income investors might want to consider to add diversification to their portfolio. Although many diverse asset classes are represented in my own Income Compounder portfolio that range from equity funds that hold mostly common stocks to bond or credit funds that hold mostly fixed income securities, preferred securities fall somewhere in-between.

This educational piece from Pimco is an excellent primer on preferred securities.

Traditional preferred and capital securities (also known as “preferreds” or “hybrids”) share the characteristics of both stocks and bonds and may offer investors higher yields than common stock or corporate bonds. These securities are perpetual and callable, typically pay dividends instead of coupons, offer multiple rate structures, often have investment-grade ratings, and are subordinated in the capital structure, as explained below.

  • Perpetual and Callable: Traditional preferreds are issued as perpetuals, i.e. with no stated maturity, but have call options typically at 5 or 10 years.
  • Dividends, Not Interest: Traditional preferreds pay dividends rather than interest and in periods of severe stress the issuer may skip a payment without triggering a default. Dividend income earned from preferreds is generally taxed at the “qualified” tax rate, which is considerably less than ordinary income tax rates that are applied to fixed income securities that pay interest.
  • Different Rate Structures: Preferreds that have more debt-like traits can have fixed rate, floating rate, or fixed-to-floating rate dividends. Floating rate structures offer significantly less interest rate risk than fixed-rate bonds.
  • Junior Ranking in the Capital Structure: Preferreds rank lower than senior debt and higher than common equity. That means, in the event of an issuer’s default, investors holding that company’s preferreds will get paid back after the bondholders and before the stockholders.
  • Higher Quality Ratings: Preferreds are often issued by investment-grade entities and even though they are typically ranked two or more notches below an issuer’s senior debt, many preferreds still garner investment-grade ratings.

Reasons to invest in preferreds include the higher yield income from what is mostly investment grade holdings, potential for tax-advantaged income (for example, when held in a taxable account the “qualified” tax rate on dividends is more favorable than the “non-qualified” tax rate on interest), low correlations to stocks, lower default risk because they are higher in the capital structure than common stocks and are generally issued by higher-rated firms, portfolio diversification, reduced interest rate risk (because they are callable and some can convert from fixed to floating rate), and lower volatility than equities.

Higher Yields

Although most preferreds are considered investment grade securities, they generally offer a higher yield than IG bonds, Treasuries, or most common stocks. High yield bonds, which are mostly “junk” rated (i.e., below investment grade) offer higher yields but come with higher risk. And because preferreds have more bond-like qualities than equity-like profiles, they are often lumped in with bonds and other fixed income investments. In this recent outlook for the 2026 market, JP Morgan (JPM) suggests that inflation will be higher and more volatile than in the past which offers a broadly positive outlook for fixed income.

Inflation has receded to below 3%, and implied bond market volatility has subsided to 2021 levels. What’s more, stock-bond correlation has declined over the last year. Sovereign bonds and investment grade credit helped hedge equity volatility during the “tariff tantrum” equity sell-off that followed the announcement of U.S. tariffs in April. That correlation decline is important for portfolio construction because it suggests that bonds can once again hedge against some equity drawdowns.

Tax Advantages of Preferred Securities

Preferred stocks typically pay qualified dividend income, or QDI, taxed at lower rates than ordinary income in most cases depending on your personal tax situation. Investopedia has more information regarding QDI if you are interested.

A dividend is considered qualified if the shareholder has held a stock for more than 60 days in the 121-day period that began 60 days before the ex-dividend date. The ex-dividend date is one market day before the dividend’s record date. The record date is when a shareholder must be on the company’s books to receive the dividend.

High Yield Income with Lower Volatility

Another benefit to holding preferred securities is that they tend to be less volatile than stocks and slightly less historically than high yield bonds. State Street Investment Management has a good educational insight about preferred securities that explains some of these benefits in more detail.

As a result of preferred shares having both bond – and stock-like features, the volatility profile of preferreds has historically been lower than that of pure common stocks, while being just a touch below that of credit-sensitive high yield bonds, as shown below. For this analysis, we used the historical median rolling 36-month standard deviation of returns over the last 15 years, as a rolling measure can account for the cyclicality within an asset class. It is also more constructive than periodic returns, as one can examine outliers. For instance, preferred shares became more volatile than both high yields bonds and US large-cap stocks during the financial crisis due to the financial concentration within the asset class. The latter point may be helpful in understanding return patterns if there is a market event that creates short-term volatility in the financial sector.

As further explained by State Street, there are a number of reasons why companies may want to issue preferred shares.

  • Issuing preferred shares requires a lower cost of capital than issuing common shares. Corporations value them as a way to obtain equity financing without diluting voting rights.
  • Preferreds give companies flexibility in making dividend payments. If a company is running into cash issues, it can suspend the dividend payments without the risk of default as would be the case with traditional bond issuance. However, if the preferred share class is cumulative (vs. non-cumulative), then the company will still have to pay back previously skipped dividends before restarting dividends in the future.
  • Preferred shares can be a balance sheet management tool, as a firm can raise capital to finance projects while not increasing its debt-to-equity ratio.
  • Preferreds are issued primarily by banks and insurance companies, and preferreds can count toward regulatory capital requirements — allowing banks to issue preferreds in order to help maintain their required capital ratios.

What are the Risks to Preferred Securities?

As explained in the Pimco report, the biggest risks to preferreds are similar to what other fixed income securities face with respect to credit risks and interest rates.

Similar to other fixed income investments, preferreds and capital securities’ performance can be affected by interest rates and credit risks. Because these instruments have direct exposure to the overall health of the banking/financial system, returns could be relatively volatile in the event of a shock to the financial markets. Preferreds and capital securities also contain risks that traditional bonds do not, including coupon deferral risk, conversion risk, and write-down risk.

In a broader market correction when all asset classes are affected like we experienced in 2022, preferred securities are likely to be negatively impacted more than IG bonds, Treasuries, or AAA CLOs (collateralized loan obligations), but less than common stock or junk-rated high yield bonds, for example. If things really go south in a hurry like they did during the Covid pandemic, preferreds are likely to see big drawdowns, but also may recover quickly as they did in 2020.

Although there are risks, Goldman Sachs believes that fixed income (including preferred issues) has positive momentum and strong fundamentals heading into 2026:

We believe the fixed income landscape in 2026 offers investors numerous opportunities to secure relatively high income streams across various asset classes. … Elsewhere, high yield credit could be another avenue for income in 2026. Credit fundamentals for high yield issuers continue to defy tariff-related fears, as evidenced by strong credit metrics like interest coverage ratios and contained default rates. The positive secular shift in the high yield ratings mix over the past decade has also enhanced the asset class's resilience during occasional macro shocks. Favorable market dynamics, including firm investor demand and easing financial conditions, should continue to support primary market activity. The recent increase in M&A activity may also be supportive. Strategic buyers, focused on long-term operational integration and expansion, rather than financial returns, may be more willing to make deals with credit-friendly financing structures that can drive price appreciation for high-yield issuers.

Some CEFs that Hold Preferreds

The fund screener on CEFConnect is useful for identifying the various CEFs (closed end funds) that hold different types of assets. There are five different funds available from Flaherty & Crumrine and four from John Hancock out of the 15 CEFs that hold Preferreds, according to the screener. Cohen & Steers offer three funds and Nuveen offers the two highest yielders, while one from First Trust rounds out the offerings with FPF.

NPFD: Newest and Highest Yielding Preferred CEF

For purposes of the Income Factory approach, funds with yields from 8% to 10% are preferable, as long as the total return does not suffer from NAV decline. Given that Nuveen Variable Rate Preferred & Income Fund (NPFD) is the highest yielding of the 15 funds selected, let’s take a closer look to see if it might be a candidate for an IF portfolio.

Unfortunately, Nuveen launched NPFD in December 2021, which turned out to be lousy timing for any CEF, one of a dozen that went public that year. I wrote a brief review in November 2023 of those 2021 CEFs that I referred to as the Dirty Dozen because all 12 performed poorly for their first two years. Here is the list of the “dirty dozen,” some of which you may recognize as potential Income Factory holdings.

  1. PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO)
  2. RiverNorth Flexible Municipal Income II (RFMZ)
  3. BlackRock Innovation and Growth Term Trust (BIGZ)
  4. Nuveen Core Plus Impact Fund (NPCT)
  5. Neuberger Berman Next Generation Connectivity Fund Inc (NBXG)
  6. Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI)
  7. Thornburg Income Builder Opportunities Trust (TBLD)
  8. Pioneer Municipal High Income Opportunities Fund Inc. (MIO)
  9. BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Term Trust (ECAT)
  10. MainStay CBRE Global Infrastructure Megatrends Term Fund (MEGI)
  11. Guggenheim Active Allocation Fund (GUG)
  12. Nuveen Variable Rate Preferred & Income Fund.

Like all 12 of the dirty dozen CEFs launched in 2021, NPFD is a term fund with a 12-year term that has the potential option to convert to perpetual. Here is the fund description from the fund website:

The Fund seeks to provide a high level of current income and total return by investing in primarily investment grade, variable rate preferred securities and other variable rate income-producing securities from high quality, highly regulated companies such as banks, utilities and insurance companies. All, or almost all, of the Fund’s distributions of net investment income are expected to be treated as qualified dividend income which is generally taxed at a lower rate than interest and ordinary dividend income, assuming holding period and certain other requirements are met.

In addition, the fund intends to invest at least 25% of managed assets in securities of companies in the financial services sector. As of October 31, 2025, the fund held 186 securities with the highest concentration in Diversified Banks as shown on this snippet from the website.

Top 10 portfolio positions include some well-known banks and insurance companies.

Currently, NPFD trades at a discount to NAV of -4% and offers a yield of 10.43% based on a monthly distribution of $0.1675 per share.

Although it has a relatively short history, NPFD increased the dividend by 40% in June 2024 where it has remained since as shown on the 3-year Dividend History chart.

Over the past 3 years, NPFD not only offers the highest distribution yield but has also delivered the best total return of the 5 highest yielding Preferred CEFs.

JPC: Distribution Increase and Fund Merger Completed

The second highest yielding of the 15 CEFs listed in the fund screener above is another Nuveen fund. The Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC) offers a 10% distribution rate, which was increased by 40% back in June 2024. JPC was originally launched back in 2003 and was merged with another Nuveen fund with a very similar name, Nuveen Preferred Securities & Income Opportunities Fund, (formerly JPI) in September of this year.

Through the merger, a wholly-owned subsidiary of JPC acquired substantially all of the assets and liabilities of JPI in a tax-free transaction, and common shares of JPI were converted to newly-issued common shares of JPC in an aggregate amount equal to the value of the net assets of JPI. The transaction took place based upon JPI’s closing net asset value on September 19, 2025.

When looking at the recent performance of JPC, the fund was trading at a wide discount for most of the past 3 years but closed the discount as the NAV rose back up to $8 (where it was in November 2022) and it now trades near par after the merger was completed.

JPC Key Facts

I have not previously analyzed JPC so let’s take a look at some key facts and data about this fund using the fact sheet to summarize. JPC holds $4.7B in managed assets, uses effective leverage of about 37%, and has 263 total holdings. Total fund expenses amount to 2.2% with 0.75% in management fees. About one third of the holdings are Contingent Capital Securities.

Contingent capital securities, sometimes called contingent convertibles (CoCos), have evolved from niche status to become a well-developed segment of the global fixed income markets. CoCos are hybrid securities created by regulators after the 2007-08 global financial crisis as a way to reduce the likelihood of government-orchestrated bailouts. Issued primarily by non-U.S. banks, CoCos are designed to automatically absorb losses, thereby helping the issuing bank satisfy Additional Tier 1 (AT1) and Tier 2 (T2) regulatory capital requirements. Today, European-domiciled issuers (mostly banks but also a small number of insurance companies) make up over 85% of the outstanding CoCo market. Insurance companies may use these securities for capital purposes or to help manage their credit ratings.

Like NPFD, the top 10 holdings in JPC as of 9/30/25 include several large banks and insurance companies as well.

Also, like NPFD, JPC substantially increased the dividend in June 2024 and now pays $0.0665 monthly.

Summary: Buy Preferred Securities for High Yield Income with Less Volatility than Stocks

In this review of the preferred securities asset class, I reviewed the basics of what they are, why income investors may want to consider adding some to help diversify their portfolio, how they can reduce volatility while providing a high yield income stream, and some potential CEFs that hold preferred securities to consider. In addition to the CEFs mentioned in this article, there are quite a few ETFs (exchange traded funds) that also hold preferreds that an income investor may want to consider.

In the screenshot below from the SA Quant ratings for Preferred Stock ETFs, you can see the ticker symbols and quant rating (for what’s it worth) for 22 of them if you would like to explore further.

data.title: Tango Therapeutics: PRMT5 Inhibitor Vopimetostat Approaches Pivotal Testing - Buy
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Tango Therapeutics, Inc. (TNGX) with a Buy rating because it has been able to do well in terms of developing its lead candidate from its pipeline, which is a PRMT5 inhibitor, vopimetostat [TNG462] for the treatment of patients with MTAP-deleted cancers. In particular, it reported positive results from the phase 1/2 study using this candidate to treat difficult, hard-to-treat 2nd-line solid tumor patients with such MTAP-deletion cancer types.

Why the "Buy" rating? That's because this company has already selected a focus on what it wants to go after with the development of this drug, which is the targeting of 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer patients. The reason why is because in this phase 1/2 study it achieved doubling of median progression-free survival [mPFS] and objective response rate [ORR] in this particular solid tumor patient population. The positive data that this company released in and of itself is bullish on its own.

However, there are two other reasons to be bullish with respect to the development of vopimetostat. The first reason is because the company has already received a go-ahead confirmation from the FDA that it could use 250 mg QD [once-daily] dosing of this drug as a monotherapy to treat 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancers. A pivotal study for this specific program targeting this group of patients is expected to start in 2026. The second reason is because the company is also studying vopimetostat in a combination phase 1/2 study targeting previously treated [2nd-line] MTAP-del/RAS mutant pancreatic and lung cancer patients. Data from this is going to show if the addition of RAS[ON] multi-selective inhibitor daraxonrasib and/or RAS[ON] G12D-selective inhibitor zodonrasib, both of which from Revolution Medicines (RVMD), are going to be good enough to treat these 2nd-line patients.

In addition, if the data is ideal enough and go-forward doses have been selected, there is a plan to add in another cohort for testing. This would be a cohort using these combinations to target patients with 1st-line MTAP-del/RAS mutant pancreatic cancer. Should this study go well, Tango Therapeutics believes that it will be able to release data from this phase 1/2 combination study in 2026. Lastly, the company is also looking to explore the use of single-agent monotherapy vopimetostat in a phase 1/2 study targeting patients with previously treated lung cancer. It is expected that data from this early-stage trial will be released in 2026 as well.

With positive results from the phase 1/2 study using vopimetostat as a monotherapy to treat patients with 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer, plus the move to a pivotal study in 2026, I believe it is important to initiate Tango Therapeutics with a Buy rating.

Targeting of MTAP-Deleted Pancreatic Cancer Patients Has Two Possible Pathways

As I noted above, Tango Therapeutics was able to achieve positive results from its phase 1/2 study targeting 2nd-line MTAP-deleted patients. Before going over the positive data released from this particular study, plus any catalysts that are to come about from this program, it is first important to understand what these types of cancer are and what the possible market opportunity for them could be. It is important to state that this phase 1/2 trial was split up into two parts as follows:

  • Open-label dose escalation portion recruiting homozygous MTAP-deleted solid tumor cancer patients.
  • Open-label dose expansion/optimization portion of the study targeting specific MTAP-deleted cancer types like [pancreatic cancer, lung cancer, etc.].

MTAP-deleted solid tumors form because of a genetic alteration that is found within them. That is, the MTAP gene is deleted from the 9p21 chromosome region. Why is this bad? That's because this deletion and that of CDKN2A are tumor suppressors, and without them the cancer is able to grow. Patients who have this type of genetic alteration [mutation] have a worse prognosis compared to those who don't have it.

On the flip side, this is a weakness that can be exploited, as these tumors rely on a molecule known as S-adenosylmethionine [SAM], which is crucial for cellular metabolism and transcription. Thus, the strategy is to inhibit this MAT2A enzyme and block the purine synthesis pathway. This leads to the killing of cancer cells and is a strategy that is deployed by this company and others.

How the heck, then, can vopimetostat help here? That's because MTAP-deletion results in vulnerability of the enzyme PRMT5. Thus, by inhibiting this vulnerable enzyme it creates an exploit that this company can use to treat these MTAP-deleted cancer patients.

As I stated above, there is the goal of using vopimetostat to target only MTAP-deleted pancreatic and lung cancer patients. This makes sense from a strategic standpoint because these are the largest populations for these types of tumors. Consider that MTAP-deletion is found in 15% of those with non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC] and then 35% in pancreatic cancer [Tango Therapeutics Corporate Presentation November 2025, slide #6]. In order to determine if this PRMT5 inhibitor would be able to treat these patients with MTAP-deleted cancers, the company evaluated them over a certain period of time in a phase 1/2 study.

It is important to note that the company achieved a positive outcome in terms of both targeting all patients and then only those specifically who had MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer. This is shown as follows with a data cutoff date of September 1, 2025, and proves why the mechanism of action [MOA] I described above works:

  • 94 evaluable patients with all histologies of MTAP-deleted solid tumors - ORR of 27% and median progression-free survival [mPFS] of 6.4 months taking doses of vopimetostat over 200 mg.
  • 39 evaluable patients with 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer ORR of 25% and mPFS of 7.2 months.

The last one is important, because as I said, the goal is to specifically go after patients with 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer. The reason why that ORR and mPFS data is also important is because it is more than double that of historical controls whereby only standard of care [SOC] is given to these patients.

This data is ideal for another reason, and that is why I have given this stock a Buy rating. It is on the basis that this company has already established a go-ahead dose with the FDA for a pivotal trial. To this end, Tango Therapeutics expects to evaluate the use of 250 mg QD [once-daily] dosing of vopimetostat in 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer patients. Such a late-stage trial is expected to start at some point in 2026.

The company doesn't just have to rely on this program of this PRMT5 inhibitor to only go after lung cancer and pancreatic cancer. What do I mean by this? Well, that's because the company ran a histology-selective cohort of 13 late-line cancers that didn't consist of these two solid tumor types [nor Sarcoma and the reason for this solid tumor type not being included is because there was no benefit found here at all with vopimetostat]. With respect to the evaluation of these 13 late-line solid tumor types with MTAP-deletion, it saw an ORR of 49% and an mPFS rate of 9.1 months.

Further Expansion Possible For Vopimetostat Towards 1st-Line MTAP-Deleted Pancreatic Cancer Patients

What I described above with respect to Tango Therapeutics developing its lead PRMT5 inhibitor vopimetostat was in terms of it as a monotherapy to target 2nd-line MTAP-deleted solid tumor types. The beauty of this company is that it is already looking towards the development of this drug in combination regimens.

To that end, it has been able to initiate a phase 1/2 study using this drug in combination with RAS[ON] inhibitors from Revolution Medicines. There is a difference with each, and it is as follows:

  • RAS[ON] multi-selective inhibitor daraxonrasib.
  • RAS[ON] G12D-selective inhibitor zoldonrasib.

What I will state right away is that even though there is a cohort from this phase 1/2 study going after previously treated MTAP-del/RAS-mutant pancreatic cancer and lung cancer patients, the goal of this study is to eventually expand towards the targeting of 1st-line MTAP-del/RAS-mutant pancreatic cancer patients. The company expects to release preliminary data of vopimetostat in combination with RAS[ON] inhibitors to target these patients in 2026. If the data is good, then the company will initiate a study targeting 1st-line patients with these mutation types in this setting in 2026.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Tango Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $152.8 million as of September 30, 2025. It is immediately important to note that the company raised $225 million in cash back on October 23, 2025. There should be no near-term risk of dilution, albeit in small bits as I will go over directly below. After enacting the cash raise bringing in $225 million, it believes it will have enough to fund its operations into 2028. This was the projection, but the company entered into a Sales Agreement with Leerink Partners LLC, where it could, from time to time, sell at its discretion up to $100 million worth of shares of its common stock.

While this could end up being dilutive to shareholders, it won't be known exactly when the company utilizes this. That is, even though this is an option in place that it can choose to use to raise cash, it doesn't have to do so.

The company burns roughly $39.7 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $30.8 million in R&D expenses and then $8.9 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Tango Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the development of vopimetostat for the treatment of patients with 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer. Even though a phase 1/2 study has shown the company to achieve a median PFS of 7.2 months and an ORR of 25% with monotherapy of this drug, there is no assurance that it will be replicated in a pivotal trial. Nor that the data to be released from such a pivotal study is going to be good enough to merit the ability for the company to file for regulatory approval of vopimetostat to treat these patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the expansion opportunity of vopimetostat with respect to it being combined with RAS[ON] inhibitors to treat both 2nd-line and 1st-line MTAP-del/RAS-mutant pancreatic and lung cancer patients in a phase 1/2 study. The two combinations being explored are vopimetostat plus daraxonrasib and vopimetostat plus zolonrasib to treat these patients. Initial safety/efficacy data utilizing both of these combinations are expected in 2026.

The risk here is that there is no assurance that the data to be released from either of these combination regimens from this phase 1/2 study are going to be positive. Nor that the data is going to be strong enough to warrant the ability for the company to move on towards pivotal trials for this particular program.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of vopimetostat for the treatment of patients with 2nd-line MTAP-deleted lung cancer for the ongoing phase 1/2 study. The overall study is expected to fully enroll a total of 41 patients; however, it is expected that data from this cohort will be released as an update in 2026.

The risk here is that even though vopimetostat as a monotherapy was shown to work well in treating patients with 2nd-line MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer and the overall solid tumor of patients, there is no assurance that a similar or superior outcome will be observed in only targeting these 2nd-line MTAP-deleted lung cancer patients.

Conclusion

Tango Therapeutics is a highly ideal biotech, and I believe that with the data it has released thus far, it merits a Buy rating. The reason why I believe that this stock should have this rating is because not only did it release positive data from its phase 1/2 study using vopimetostat to treat 2nd-line patients with MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer, but also because it is already set to start a pivotal study for this program in 2026. That is, it expects to initiate a late-stage trial using this PRMT5 inhibitor as a monotherapy to treat these patients with previously treated MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancers.

Furthermore, what's encouraging is that the company can expand the scope of vopimetostat beyond that of only targeting patients with MTAP-deleted pancreatic cancer and lung cancer. This would be a histology-agnostic study done treating 13 cancer types with MTAP-deletion using vopimeteostat. It is important to state that the company excluded lung cancer and pancreatic cancer from this test [because it is already exploring them in specific histology cohorts] and also excluded sarcoma solid tumor types [the reason for this is because with 9 patients being treated with this drug, no responses were observed].

Data from this cohort is encouraging and lends credibility for it to eventually expand towards other solid tumor types with MTAP-deletion should it choose to do so. Consider that this specific cohort of patients given this drug achieved an ORR of 49% and an mPFS rate of 9.1 months. With positive data from the phase 1/2 study using vopimetostat to treat 2nd-line MTAP-deletion pancreatic cancer patients, plus the ability to combine it with either RAS[ON] inhibitors, daraxonrasib or zoldonrasib to expand to 1st-line MTAP-del/RAS-mutant pancreatic and lung cancer patients, I believe that this stock should be initiated with a Buy Rating.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
